Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, usually studying in the evenings after my part-time work and on weekends. I'd dedicate around 29 hours a week, which sounds like a lot, but with nine online courses it felt necessary. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to recorded lectures while doing other things or replay them multiple times. Honestly, some weeks were better organized than others, but I tried to keep a basic structure going.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but it definitely shifted when deadlines approached. I'd try to spread things out during the week, but sometimes life got in the way and I'd end up cramming more toward the end. With limited resources, I couldn't always afford to be in ideal study conditions, so I had to be flexible about when and how I studied.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check on assignment deadlines or see if there were new lecture recordings posted. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who logged in constantly—maybe about five times a week—but I tried to make each session count for something specific.
Q4: It took a while, but eventually checking the LMS became part of my evening routine, usually after dinner. I'd sit down with my laptop and just go through what needed attention. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me be more intentional about this—I'd take a few deep breaths and then approach the platform more calmly instead of feeling overwhelmed by everything waiting for me there.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them while commuting or doing chores, which helped me maximize my limited time. The discussion forums were also useful because hearing other students' perspectives through their written words helped concepts click for me. I participated in about 46 peer interactions, which really enriched my understanding.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the reading materials, especially lengthy PDFs that required a lot of visual focus. With my auditory learning style, reading dense text just doesn't stick as well. I also didn't engage much with optional practice quizzes because they felt less urgent than the actual assignments. Time was precious and I had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. I also valued activities that I could engage with through listening rather than just reading. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my learning goals, I'd often skip it or rush through it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With required work, there was no question—I had to do it. But optional stuff? I'd weigh whether I had the energy and time, and often the answer was no. That said, when I did engage with optional materials, it was because I genuinely wanted to understand something better, which made those moments feel more personally meaningful.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it offline constantly—on the bus, while cooking, even while falling asleep sometimes. I also took handwritten notes and would review those instead of going back to the platform. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd be walking around my apartment listening to a downloaded lecture, pausing and rewinding to really absorb points. Or I'd be discussing course concepts with classmates over coffee, working through problems together. None of that shows up as platform activity, but it was real learning.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach actually. I'd watch or download a video once, then continue engaging with it offline for days afterward. I'd listen to the audio multiple times during my commute or while doing housework. The initial access was just the starting point—the real engagement happened offline where I could learn at my own pace without worrying about internet connectivity.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. I was feeling quite sad during parts of the course, and my motivation was pretty low. Some days it was hard to even open the laptop. The mindfulness intervention helped lift my mood eventually, but there were stretches where engaging felt like pushing through thick fog.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. With limited resources, I sometimes had to prioritize work over studying, which left me exhausted. When I was tired, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely meant my task completion wasn't as high as it could have been—around 50%—even though I was trying my best.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or just feeling that persistent sadness. I'd technically be 'watching' the content, but I wasn't really present. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions without actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments even when I knew they were important, especially when I was feeling low. The sadness made everything feel harder than it should be. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but sometimes tomorrow wasn't much better. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this—only about 30%—even though my actual assignment scores were good when I did submit.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver. Without them, I might have drifted even more than I did. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly find the energy to focus and produce decent work. My exam score of 78 and good assignment scores show that I could perform well under pressure. The structure of deadlines helped compensate for my low motivation.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes it took a while to sink in. Positive feedback gave me a small boost and made me feel like my efforts were worth something. Critical feedback was harder—with my low resilience, it could send me into a bit of a spiral. But I tried to use it constructively, especially after starting the mindfulness practice which helped me respond rather than react.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having regular deadlines kept me on track, but having nine online courses simultaneously was overwhelming. The moderate engagement level the course expected was manageable, but I often felt stretched thin. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized so I knew exactly what was expected each week.
Q19: Having recorded lectures made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. I could replay difficult sections and learn at my own pace. What made it harder was when important information was only in text format or when there were too many different resources to keep track of. Simplicity and audio options made everything more accessible for me.
Q20: I think sometimes students are searching for something—trying to find resources that click with their learning style, or just feeling lost and clicking around hoping something helps. High activity might also mean someone is struggling to understand and keeps revisiting materials without making progress. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying; they might just be stuck.
Q21: This actually describes me somewhat. My LMS activity was moderate, but I performed reasonably well on exams and assignments. That's because so much of my real studying happened offline—listening to downloaded lectures, reviewing my notes, discussing with peers. The platform only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course material.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, what you click on, and whether you meet deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional states, or the depth of understanding. They see the surface, not what's underneath.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being present and curious. For me, engagement looks like listening to a lecture and pausing to think about how concepts connect, or discussing ideas with classmates because I genuinely want to understand better.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester, or how the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped improve my mood by the end. It wouldn't capture all those hours listening to lectures while commuting, or the peer conversations that happened outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling sad and unmotivated, I still pushed through and earned a Merit grade.
